chapters.title: Module 1: The Dawn of PySpark – Taming Big Data
chapters.description: The Dawn of PySpark – Taming Big Data imported from DOCX source.

blocks.content:
The Catalyst: Web 2.0 and the Data Avalanche
Before we can understand what PySpark is, we must understand why it had to be invented.
In the late 1990s and early 2000s, data was manageable. Companies relied on traditional Relational Database Management Systems (RDBMS) like Oracle, SQL Server, and MySQL. These systems organized data into clean rows and columns and worked extremely well — as long as the data was structured and limited in scale.
Then Web 2.0 happened.
The internet transformed from static pages into interactive platforms. Companies were no longer storing only customer records — they were capturing every search query, every click, every scroll, every interaction.
This was the birth of Big Data.
The Breaking Point: Scaling Up vs. Scaling Out
Traditional databases were built on Vertical Scaling (Scaling Up).
When performance dropped, you bought a bigger machine:
More RAM
More CPU
Larger disks
Eventually, this approach hit a physical and financial wall.
Data was growing exponentially.Machine power was growing linearly.
The industry needed a paradigm shift.
Instead of one $1,000,000 supercomputer:

Traditional SQL systems were not designed for this.
The 3 Vs of Big Data
Big Data wasn’t just “more data.” It was fundamentally different.
1. Volume
From gigabytes to petabytes.From millions of rows to trillions.
A single large company could generate billions of records per day.
2. Velocity
Data stopped arriving in nightly batches.It began streaming 24/7.
Credit card transactions.IoT sensors.User clicks.
Systems had to react in near real time.
3. Variety
Data was no longer neatly structured.It arrived as:
JSON
Logs
Text
Images
Audio
Rigid schemas could not keep up.
Course Anchor
In this course, PySpark will become the engine that solves these constraints — not theoretically, but operationally. You will not just understand distributed systems. You will build them.
Deep Dive: Big Data in the Smart Power Grid
Consider one utility company monitoring 1 million smart meters.
Each meter sends a reading every 15 minutes.
That equals:
96 million records per day.
Over time, this becomes terabytes of data annually.
But storing data is not the hard part.
The real challenges are Veracity and Value.
4. Veracity – When Data Lies
Massive scale becomes dangerous when the data is wrong.
Imagine:
Network dropouts during a storm
A faulty smart meter reporting 10,000 kW usage for a small home
A cyberattack injecting fake voltage readings
If automated systems react blindly, power lines shut down.Hundreds of thousands of homes lose electricity.
This is not a theoretical bug.It becomes a national headline.
The Engineering Challenge
You cannot manually validate 96 million daily records.
You need distributed validation pipelines that:
Detect impossible values
Impute missing readings
Standardize inconsistent formats
The PySpark Connection
In this course you will use PySpark DataFrames to:
Filter physically impossible sensor readings across clusters
Apply Window Functions to fill missing time-series data
Build clean “gold” tables for operators
5. Value – Turning Data Into Stability
Clean data alone has zero business impact.
Value means prediction.
A traditional grid reacts after a transformer fails.A smart grid predicts failure before it happens.
This requires combining:
Real-time consumption
10 years of historical load data
Weather signals
Running these simulations on a single machine would crash it.
The PySpark Connection
Later in this course, you will explore:
Spark MLlib for predictive modeling
Spark Structured Streaming for near real-time anomaly detection
The Hadoop Era
Before Spark, there was Hadoop.
Hadoop introduced:
HDFS (distributed storage)
MapReduce (distributed computation)
It solved Volume and Variety.
But it had limitations.
Disk-Bound Processing
MapReduce wrote intermediate results to disk after every step.
Machine learning requires iterating over the same data repeatedly.Disk I/O made this painfully slow.
Batch-Only Architecture
Hadoop was designed for overnight jobs.
Modern systems required real-time responses.Hadoop could not natively handle streaming data.
Developer Friction
MapReduce required verbose Java code.
It was built for engineers — not analysts.
The Spark Revolution
Spark changed distributed computing fundamentally.
In-Memory Processing
Instead of writing to disk between steps, Spark caches data in RAM.
For iterative algorithms, this can be up to 100x faster than MapReduce.
Unified Engine
Spark provides:
Spark SQL & DataFrames
Structured Streaming
MLlib
All inside one engine.
The Rise of PySpark
Spark exposed high-level APIs in Python.
This allowed data professionals to write distributed programs without learning Java or Scala.
Modern Deployment: The Cloud Shift
Today, Spark runs primarily on managed platforms:
Databricks
Amazon EMR
Google Cloud Dataproc
Clusters are provisioned automatically.Scaling is abstracted.
Your focus becomes code — not hardware.
Code Teaser
In the next module, you will understand what truly happens when you execute:

That single filter may execute across 100 machines.
Module 1 Checkpoint
You now understand:
Why traditional databases failed
Why horizontal scaling became mandatory
Why Hadoop was a stepping stone
Why Spark dominates modern distributed analytics
Why PySpark democratizes distributed systems
In Module 2, we move from history to architecture.
You will see what actually happens inside a Spark cluster when you run your first job.

chapters.title: Module 2: Up and Running – Your First PySpark DataFrame
chapters.description: Estimated Time: 120 MinutesModule Objective: Initialize a PySpark environment, deeply understand lazy evaluation, and perform foundational DataFrame operations using a distributed systems mindset.

sections.title: Lesson 1: SparkSession – Entering the Distributed World (30 mins)

blocks.content:
Before processing distributed data, you must initialize a Spark application.
Local development:
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="003F4F8D"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>pip install pyspark
In enterprise environments, Spark typically runs inside:
Databricks
Amazon EMR
Google Dataproc
Kubernetes clusters
Your laptop runs Spark in local mode (a simulation). Production Spark runs across real clusters.
Understanding this distinction matters.
The SparkSession – The Application Boundary

What this really means:
The SparkSession runs on the Driver process.
The Driver builds logical plans.
Executors run tasks across partitions.
You are not just creating a Python object.You are connecting to a distributed execution engine.

Aggregations – groupBy()

<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="003F4F8D"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>from pyspark.sql.functions import sum

<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="003F4F8D"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t xml:space="preserve"> .show()
This triggers a shuffle — data must move across partitions.
Shuffle is expensive.
Understanding Actions That Hurt
count()
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="003F4F8D"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>df.count()
This scans the entire dataset.
On terabytes of data, this is not "just a quick check".
collect()
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="003F4F8D"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>df.collect()
This brings all data to the Driver.
On large datasets, this crashes memory.
Rule:
Never collect large distributed datasets unless absolutely necessary.
Caching and Persistence
If you reuse a DataFrame multiple times:
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="003F4F8D"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>df.cache()
Spark stores intermediate results in memory.
Useful when:
Iterative ML tasks
Repeated aggregations
Always measure before caching.
Partition Awareness
Spark splits data into partitions.
A partition is the smallest unit of parallelism in Spark.Each partition is processed by one task on one executor core.
If you have:
8 partitions → at most 8 tasks can run in parallel
200 partitions → up to 200 tasks can run (depending on cluster size)
So partitions directly control parallelism.
Inspect Current Partition Count
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="003F4F8D"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>df.rdd.getNumPartitions()
This tells you how many parallel chunks your DataFrame is currently divided into.
In local mode, this is often small.In production, this can be hundreds or thousands.
When Do You Need to Adjust Partitions?
1. Too Few Partitions (Underutilization)
Symptoms:
CPU usage is low
Tasks take a long time
Cluster resources are idle
Fix:
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="003F4F8D"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>df = df.repartition(200)
This increases parallelism.
⚠️ Note: repartition() causes a full shuffle — data is redistributed across the cluster.
Use it when:
Preparing for large joins
Writing output evenly across files
Increasing parallel processing
2. Too Many Partitions (Overhead)
Symptoms:
Thousands of tiny tasks
High scheduling overhead
Small output files problem
Fix:
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="003F4F8D"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>df = df.coalesce(10)
coalesce() reduces partitions without a full shuffle (when decreasing partition count).
Use it when:
Writing final output
Reducing small files
Consolidating data after filtering
repartition() vs coalesce()
Method
Causes Shuffle
Typical Use Case
repartition()
Yes
Increase partitions or rebalance data
coalesce()
No (when reducing)
Reduce partitions efficiently
Why Partitioning Matters
Partitioning strategy affects:
Parallelism – how many tasks run simultaneously
Performance – balanced partitions prevent stragglers
Resource utilization – full CPU usage vs idle cores
Shuffle cost – unnecessary repartitioning can be expensive
A poorly partitioned dataset can make a powerful cluster feel slow.A well-partitioned dataset can make moderate hardware perform efficiently.
Partitioning is not an advanced tweak.It is a core performance lever in distributed systems.
Writing Data
Saving results:
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="003F4F8D"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>projected_df.write \
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="003F4F8D"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t xml:space="preserve"> .mode("overwrite") \
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="003F4F8D"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:lastRenderedPageBreak/><w:t xml:space="preserve"> .parquet("/output/smart_grid_projection/")
Write is an Action.
Spark writes data distributed — multiple files, one per partition.
Architectural Recap
By now, you should understand:
Driver builds logical plans
Executors process partitions
Transformations are lazy
Actions trigger execution
Shuffle is expensive
Partitioning impacts performance
collect() is dangerous
You are not writing scripts.
You are defining distributed execution graphs.
Module 2 Checkpoint
You now know how to:
Initialize Spark
Create and read DataFrames
Understand lazy evaluation
Inspect execution plans
Perform transformations and aggregations
Avoid common performance traps
Manage partitions and caching
Write distributed output
In Module 3, we go deeper.
We open the engine.
You will understand:
DAG Scheduler
Catalyst Optimizer
Shuffle mechanics
Tungsten execution engine
From here forward, you are thinking like a distributed systems engineer.

chapters.title: Module 3: Inside the Engine – Spark Internals for Distributed Engineers
chapters.description: Welcome to Module 3. By now, you've learned how to initialize Spark, create DataFrames, apply lazy evaluation, and reason about partitions. You’ve seen how poor partitioning can cripple performance and how good partitioning can unlock parallelism. Now we go deeper. We open the engine. You are no longer just writing Python.From here forward, you are thinking like a distributed systems engineer. What You Will Master in This Module Catalyst Optimizer DAG Scheduler Shuffle mechanics Project Tungsten execution engine Let’s power up.

sections.title: Lesson 1: Catalyst Optimizer – The Brain of Spark

blocks.content:
When you write PySpark code, you are not telling Spark how to execute.You are describing what result you want.
Catalyst is Spark’s query optimization engine.
When you chain transformations like:
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00E7578C"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>result = df.filter(df.customer_type == "Residential") \
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00E7578C"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t xml:space="preserve"> .groupBy("date") \
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00E7578C"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t xml:space="preserve"> .sum("daily_kw")
Spark does not immediately execute this.
Instead, Catalyst processes your query in multiple phases.
Phase 1: Unresolved Logical Plan
Spark parses your code.
At this stage, column references may not yet be validated.
Phase 2: Resolved Logical Plan
Spark checks:
Do referenced columns exist?
Are types valid?
Are functions registered?
Now the plan is structurally valid.
Phase 3: Optimized Logical Plan
This is where the intelligence happens.
Catalyst applies rule-based optimizations such as:
Predicate pushdown
Constant folding
Projection pruning
Join reordering
Example:
If you filter after a join:
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00E7578C"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>df1.join(df2, "meter_id") \
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00E7578C"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t xml:space="preserve"> .filter(df1.customer_type == "Residential")
Catalyst may push the filter before the join.
That reduces memory usage and shuffle volume dramatically.
Phase 4: Physical Plan
Catalyst generates multiple physical strategies:
Broadcast join
Sort-merge join
Hash aggregation
It selects the lowest estimated cost plan.
Inspect it with:
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00E7578C"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>result.explain("formatted")
Understanding this output separates engineers from script writers.

sections.title: Lesson 2: The DAG Scheduler – The Blueprint of Execution

blocks.content:
Once Catalyst selects a Physical Plan, it is handed to the DAG Scheduler.
Spark builds a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of execution.
Jobs
A Job is created when you trigger an Action:
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00E7578C"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>result.show()
One Action → One Job.
Stages
A Job is divided into Stages.
A new stage begins when Spark encounters a shuffle boundary.
Narrow transformations (map, filter) stay within a stage.Wide transformations (groupBy, join) create new stages.
Tasks
Each stage is split into Tasks.
One task per partition.One task runs on one executor core.
If you have 500 partitions → 500 tasks.
Parallelism equals partition count (bounded by cluster cores).

Shuffle occurs when data must move across partitions.
Example:
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00E7578C"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>df.groupBy("customer_type").sum("daily_kw")
All records with the same key must be brought together.
That means:
Writing intermediate data
Network transfer
Sorting
Repartitioning
Shuffle is expensive because it involves disk + network IO.
Why Minimizing Shuffle Matters
Excessive shuffle causes:
Slow jobs
Executor memory pressure
Skewed tasks
Stragglers
Your job as a Spark engineer:
Minimize shuffle.When unavoidable, balance partitions.
Data Skew Example
If 90% of records belong to "Residential" and only 10% to others,
One partition may receive almost all data.
That executor becomes a bottleneck.
This is called data skew.
Mitigation techniques include:
Salting keys
Repartitioning intelligently
Broadcast joins (when small table exists)

If Catalyst is the brain and DAG Scheduler is the blueprint,Project Tungsten is the raw execution muscle.
Early Hadoop systems were disk-bound.Spark moved execution into RAM.
Tungsten goes further.
1. Memory Management
Spark bypasses traditional JVM object storage.
Instead of heavy Java objects, it uses compact binary formats.
This reduces garbage collection overhead.
2. Cache-Aware Computation
Data is organized to align with CPU cache lines.
This improves hardware-level efficiency.
3. Whole-Stage Code Generation
Spark dynamically generates optimized bytecode.
Instead of interpreting row operations, it compiles them.
This dramatically improves performance.
Performance Mental Model
When a Spark job runs:
You define transformations.
Catalyst optimizes the logical plan.
A physical plan is generated.
DAG Scheduler splits into stages.
Tasks execute across partitions.
Shuffle moves data when required.
Tungsten executes operations at hardware speed.
That is the full pipeline.
Module 3 Checkpoint
You now understand:
How Catalyst rewrites your queries
How DAG Scheduler organizes execution
What triggers shuffle
Why shuffle is expensive
How Tungsten improves hardware efficiency
From this point forward, you are not learning Spark syntax.
You are analyzing distributed execution behavior.
In the next module, we will deliberately create inefficient queries,force shuffles, analyze execution plans, and fix performance issues step by step.

chapters.title: Module 4: Performance Tuning – Finding and Fixing Bottlenecks
chapters.description: Estimated Time: 120 MinutesModule Objective: Learn how to systematically diagnose slow or crashing PySpark jobs, understand the real distributed causes behind them, and apply foundational performance tuning strategies with clarity and confidence. Why This Module Matters Up to this point: You know how Spark works. You understand Catalyst, DAG Scheduler, shuffle, and partitions. Now comes the real-world question: "Why did my job take 40 minutes yesterday and 4 hours today?" Performance tuning is not guesswork.It is structured diagnosis. This module teaches you how to think like a performance engineer.

sections.title: Lesson 1: Identifying the Bottleneck – Why Did My Job Crash?

blocks.content:
When Spark jobs fail or run slowly, the root cause is usually one of five things:
Excessive shuffle
Data skew
Memory pressure
Driver overload
Poor partitioning
Let’s break these down clearly.
The "Spill to Disk" Problem
Spark is designed for in-memory processing.
But when an executor does not have enough memory to complete a task (often during shuffle or aggregation), Spark temporarily writes intermediate data to disk.
This is called spilling.
Why Spilling Is Dangerous
RAM speed: nanosecondsDisk speed: milliseconds
That difference is enormous.
If your job constantly spills to disk, performance collapses.
When Does Spill Happen?
Typical scenarios:
Large groupBy() on high-cardinality columns
Wide joins
Very large partitions
Insufficient executor memory
Example:
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00FE324A"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>df.groupBy("meter_id").sum("daily_kw")
If meter_id has millions of unique values, aggregation state grows rapidly.
In Spark UI, you may see:
"Memory Bytes Spilled"
"Disk Bytes Spilled"
High numbers here = memory bottleneck.
OutOfMemory (OOM) – Driver vs Executor
There are two types of OOM errors.
1. Driver OOM
Most commonly caused by:
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00FE324A"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:lastRenderedPageBreak/><w:t>df.collect()
If the dataset is 10GB and the Driver has 4GB RAM → crash.
The Driver is not distributed.It cannot magically scale.
Safer alternatives:
df.limit(20).show()
df.sample(0.01).collect()
Writing results to storage
2. Executor OOM
Occurs when a single task processes too much data.
Common causes:
Data skew
Large shuffle partitions
Huge joins
Executor OOM often appears during shuffle-heavy stages.

sections.title: Lesson 2: The Anatomy of a Shuffle – The Real Bottleneck

blocks.content:
Shuffle happens when Spark must redistribute data across partitions.
Operations that trigger shuffle:
groupBy()
join()
distinct()
repartition()
orderBy()
What Actually Happens During Shuffle?
Step-by-step:
Each partition processes local data.
Records are re-partitioned by key.
Intermediate data is written to disk.
Data is transferred over the network.
Receiving executors merge and sort incoming data.
This involves:
Disk IO
Network IO
Serialization
Sorting
Shuffle is expensive because it touches every slow system component.
Concrete Example
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00FE324A"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>df.groupBy("customer_type").sum("daily_kw")
If you have 1 billion rows across 200 partitions:
Each partition processes ~5 million rows.
Then all rows must be regrouped by customer_type.
Spark reshuffles data so identical keys land in the same partition.
This is unavoidable — but it must be controlled.
Data Skew – The Silent Performance Killer
Data skew occurs when one key dominates.
Example distribution:
Residential → 900 million rows
Commercial → 80 million rows
Industrial → 20 million rows
After shuffle:
One partition receives 900 million rows.Other partitions receive tiny amounts.
Result:
One task runs for 30 minutes.
Others finish in 2 minutes.
The entire stage waits for the slowest task.
This is called a straggler.

sections.title: Lesson 3: Reading Execution Plans Like an Engineer

blocks.content:
Use:

Execution plans are read bottom-up.
Bottom:
Data source scan (Parquet, CSV, etc.)
Middle:
Filters
Projections
Upper layers:
Exchanges (shuffle)
Aggregations
Joins
Top:
Final output
The Red Flag: Exchange
If you see:
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00FE324A"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>Exchange hashpartitioning
That means shuffle.
Multiple Exchanges in one query?
You may be shuffling repeatedly.
Example Plan Interpretation
If plan shows:
Scan Parquet
Filter
Exchange
HashAggregate
It means:
Data was read
Filter applied
Data shuffled
Aggregated
Understanding this sequence tells you exactly where cost lies.

sections.title: Lesson 4: Practical Fixes – Theory Behind the Optimization

blocks.content:
Now we apply distributed reasoning.
Fix 1: Broadcast Joins
Problem:
Large table joined with small table.
Example:
meter_readings → 1 TB
zip_codes → 5 MB
Why does the default join cause shuffle on both sides?
In a normal (shuffle) join, Spark must ensure that rows with the same join key end up in the same partition.
If both tables are already partitioned differently, Spark cannot assume matching keys are colocated.
So it does the following:
Re-partitions both tables by the join key.
Writes intermediate shuffle data to disk.
Transfers that data across the network.
Reassembles matching keys on target executors.
When one table is 1 TB, shuffling it across the network is extremely expensive.
What does broadcasting change?
When we explicitly broadcast the small table:
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00FE324A"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00FE324A"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>meter_readings.join(broadcast(zip_codes), "zip_code")
Spark performs a different physical strategy:
The 5 MB table is copied into memory on every executor.
The 1 TB table remains in its original partitions.
Each executor performs the join locally against its partition of the large table.
There is no need to reshuffle the 1 TB dataset.
This eliminates massive network and disk IO.
Broadcast joins are ideal when:
One table is very small (fits comfortably in executor memory).
The large table is already reasonably partitioned.
The join key is not severely skewed.
In distributed systems, avoiding movement of large datasets is one of the most powerful optimization strategies.
How small is “small” for broadcast?
Spark decides broadcastability primarily by estimated table size in bytes, not by row count.
By default, Spark may auto-broadcast a table if its estimated size is under:
spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold (commonly 10 MB by default in Spark 3.x)
You can check it with:
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00FE324A"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>spark.conf.get("spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold")
Why row count is a tricky metric
“10 MB” could be:
Millions of rows if the table has only a few small columns (e.g., zip_code, city)
Tens of thousands of rows if the table has wide strings, arrays, or many columns
So the right question isn’t “how many rows?”It’s:
“How many bytes will be replicated to every executor?”
Because broadcasting means:
The small table is copied into memory on every executor
If you have 30 executors and a 50 MB table, that’s ~1.5 GB of replicated memory cluster-wide.
Practical rules of thumb (what students should remember)
≤ 10 MB: usually safe and often auto-broadcast
10–50 MB: usually safe in healthy clusters
50–200 MB: depends on executor memory + concurrency
> 200 MB: rarely worth broadcasting
A simple sanity check:
broadcast_size × num_executors should not consume a meaningful chunk of executor memory under load.
Is it usually a “contextual” DataFrame?
Yes.
Broadcast joins are most commonly used for small contextual tables such as:
Dimension / lookup tables (zip codes, customer segment, meter metadata)
Reference mappings (tariff codes, region mapping)
Small configuration tables (thresholds, rule tables)
In a star-schema mindset:
Large table = fact (meter readings)
Small table = dimension (customer info, locations)
That’s the classic broadcast join scenario.
Concrete row-count examples (just to build intuition)
These are rough examples because row size varies:
Zip code mapping (e.g., 50k–200k rows, 3–6 short columns) → often broadcastable
Country/region mapping (200–2k rows) → always broadcastable
Customer segment lookup (100k–5M rows, narrow columns) → sometimes broadcastable
If the table contains wide string columns (long descriptions) or nested structures, the “safe row count” drops fast.
Fix 2: Handling Data Skew with Salting
Problem:
One key dominates.
Example scenario:
You have 1 billion records:
Residential → 900M
Commercial → 80M
Industrial → 20M
When you run:
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00FE324A"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>df.groupBy("customer_type").sum("daily_kw")
During shuffle, Spark hashes by customer_type.
All 900M Residential rows go to the same partition.
Result:
One executor processes 900M rows
Others process small amounts
One task becomes a straggler
Concept:
Artificially split heavy keys into multiple pseudo-keys so Spark distributes work.
Concrete Example (Step-by-Step)
Step 1: Add salt column
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00FE324A"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>from pyspark.sql.functions import rand, floor
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00FE324A"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>salted_df = df.withColumn("salt", floor(rand() * 10))
Now each Residential record randomly gets a salt value from 0 to 9.
Step 2: Create salted key
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00FE324A"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>from pyspark.sql.functions import col, concat

<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00FE324A"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t xml:space="preserve"> "salted_key",
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00FE324A"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t xml:space="preserve"> concat(col("customer_type"), col("salt"))
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00FE324A"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>)
Instead of one key "Residential", you now have:
Residential0
Residential1
Residential2
...
Residential9
The 900M rows are now roughly split into ~90M per salted key.
Step 3: First aggregation (parallelized)
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00FE324A"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>partial = salted_df.groupBy("salted_key").sum("daily_kw")
Now Spark distributes 10 heavy groups instead of 1.
Step 4: Remove salt and re-aggregate
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00FE324A"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>final = partial.withColumn(
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00FE324A"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t xml:space="preserve"> "customer_type",
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00FE324A"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t xml:space="preserve"> col("salted_key").substr(1, 11) # remove numeric salt
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00FE324A"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:lastRenderedPageBreak/><w:t>).groupBy("customer_type").sum("sum(daily_kw)")
This final aggregation is tiny compared to the original skewed shuffle.
Why this works:
Instead of one executor processing 900M rows,
10 executors process ~90M each.
Total work stays the same.Parallelism increases dramatically.
Trade-off:
Slightly more computation
Additional aggregation step
But far better cluster utilization.
Fix 3: Managing Partitions Intelligently
Partition count affects:
Parallelism
Shuffle size
Task duration
Example 1: Too Few Partitions
Suppose:
100-core cluster
DataFrame has 8 partitions
Only 8 tasks run in parallel.92 cores sit idle.
Fix:
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00FE324A"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>df = df.repartition(200)
Now up to 100 cores can work simultaneously.
Example 2: Too Many Partitions
Suppose:
10GB dataset
10,000 partitions
Each partition ≈ 1MB.
Spark must schedule 10,000 tasks.Scheduler overhead increases.Writing output creates 10,000 tiny files.
Fix:
<w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="916"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="1832"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="2748"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="3664"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="4580"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="5496"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="6412"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="7328"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="8244"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="9160"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10076"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="10992"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="11908"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="12824"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="13740"/><w:tab w:val="left" w:pos="14656"/></w:tabs><w:spacing w:line="240" w:lineRule="auto"/><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr></w:pPr><w:r w:rsidRPr="00FE324A"><w:rPr><w:rFonts w:ascii="Courier New" w:eastAsia="Times New Roman" w:hAnsi="Courier New" w:cs="Courier New"/><w:sz w:val="20"/><w:szCs w:val="20"/><w:lang w:val="en-LT" w:eastAsia="en-GB"/></w:rPr><w:t>df = df.coalesce(200)
Reduces overhead and avoids full shuffle.
When to Use Each
Use repartition() when:
Increasing parallelism
Preparing for large joins
Redistributing skewed data
Use coalesce() when:
Reducing output files
Consolidating data after heavy filtering
Final write stage
Partition strategy directly affects shuffle cost and cluster efficiency.
Fix 3: Managing Partitions Intelligently
Partition count affects:
Parallelism
Shuffle size
Task duration
Too few partitions:
Underutilized cluster
Too many partitions:
Scheduler overhead
Small file problem
Use:
repartition() when increasing parallelism (shuffle happens)
coalesce() when reducing partitions efficiently
Performance Debugging Mental Checklist
When a job runs slowly, ask:
Is there excessive shuffle?
Is data skew present?
Are partitions balanced?
Is memory spilling occurring?
Is Driver overloaded?
Performance tuning is systematic elimination of bottlenecks.
Module 4 Checkpoint
You now understand:
Why spilling happens
Difference between Driver and Executor OOM
What shuffle truly does internally
Why data skew creates stragglers
How to read execution plans
When to use broadcast joins
When to apply salting
How partition strategy impacts performance
You are no longer surprised when Spark slows down.
You can diagnose it.
And soon — you will fix it hands-on.

chapters.title: Module 5: Advanced Joins & The Art of Distributed Relationships
chapters.description: Welcome to Module 5. You have already mastered: The Brain (Catalyst) The Muscle (Tungsten) The Diagnostics (Performance Tuning) Now we master relationships at scale. In distributed systems, a join is not just a SQL keyword.It is coordinated network movement, memory allocation, and partition realignment. When you join 1 TB with 500 GB, you are not combining tables.You are orchestrating cluster-wide data movement. This module focuses on understanding the physical reality of joins.

sections.title: Lesson 1: The Shuffle Hash Join – The Standard Workhorse

blocks.content:
When you join two large DataFrames that cannot fit in memory, Spark typically performs a Shuffle Hash Join or Sort-Merge Join.
What Actually Happens
Both tables are redistributed (shuffled) across the cluster based on the join key.
Rows with identical keys land in the same partition.
Each executor performs the join locally.
ASCII Visualization – Shuffle Join

Why It’s Expensive
If you join:
meter_readings → 1 TB
weather_history → 500 GB
Spark may move up to 1.5 TB across the network.
Network IO + Disk IO + Sorting = High Cost.

sections.title: Lesson 2: Broadcast Joins – Skipping the Shuffle

blocks.content:
Instead of shuffling both tables:
The small table is copied to every executor.
The large table remains partitioned.
Join happens locally.

Mental Model
Shuffle Join → Move Both TablesBroadcast Join → Move Small Table Only
Broadcast is ideal for:
Dimension tables
Lookup mappings
Metadata enrichment
Always think in terms of bytes moved across the network.

Self-joins are often expensive because they introduce shuffle.
Example self-join logic:
